Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd have bursts where I'd watch a bunch of videos in one sitting, maybe over three hours, then not touch anything for days. I tried to set up a schedule at the start of the semester but it fell apart pretty quickly. With limited resources and having to juggle other stuff, I just studied whenever I could grab the time, which wasn't always consistent.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving - I'd cram before something was due and then coast afterward. Regular times just didn't work for me with my extracurriculars and the fact that I was always dealing with something. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks almost nothing.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or just boredom, to be honest. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something and that would remind me to check in. Other times I'd log in just to see what I was missing, scroll through a bit, watch some videos, and then log off without doing much actual work. It wasn't always productive.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of logging in most mornings with my coffee. I'd check the discussion boards and watch whatever new content was posted. But that routine broke down when things got busy with my other activities. After that, it was more sporadic and I never really got back to that consistency.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could rewatch sections I didn't understand and pause when I needed to think. I also found some of the peer interactions helpful when people explained concepts differently than the instructor. Those made the material click better than just reading through text-heavy resources.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and some of the discussion prompts. The readings felt dense and time-consuming, and I figured I could get the gist from the videos anyway. Some of the optional practice exercises I skipped too because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already stretched thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was going to directly help me pass an exam or complete an assignment. Visual stuff like diagrams or video explanations were engaging. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to what I'd be tested on, I'd probably skip it or skim through it quickly.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required stuff I'd force myself to do even if I wasn't feeling it, but optional things? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and then never did. It's not that I didn't see their value, I just had to prioritize what actually counted for my grade.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and videos to my laptop so I could study without needing internet, which isn't always reliable for me. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual studying happened offline with my notes spread out on my bed.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded materials or going over my handwritten notes for hours, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other or explaining concepts, and none of that peer learning showed up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that would be my main study material for days or even weeks. I didn't see the point in streaming the same video multiple times when I could just have it saved. So my LMS activity looked minimal even when I was actually reviewing stuff regularly.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars and I was feeling pretty drained. Even though my stress wasn't super high, I just felt disconnected from the course. The material started piling up and I fell behind on assignments, which made it even harder to catch up.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or had competing responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd log in with good intentions but end up just scrolling without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections entirely instead of pushing through them.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while I was doing something else, or I'd click through slides without really reading them. Technically I was accessing the materials but my mind was elsewhere. It looked like engagement but it was really just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was procrastination, part of it was feeling overwhelmed by where to even start. Sometimes I'd convince myself I'd do better work if I waited until I was in the right headspace, but that headspace never really came.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have a huge spike in LMS activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing to finish. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and not engage much at all. It wasn't healthy but it's how I operated.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did motivate me to try harder on the next thing. But honestly, feedback was often delayed or generic, so it didn't always feel relevant by the time I saw it. If I got encouraging comments, I'd engage more with similar activities afterward.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I needed more external structure to stay on track. When modules were clearly laid out with deadlines, I did better. When things were too open-ended, I drifted.
Q19: The video content being well-organized made engagement easier - I could see what I needed to watch and check things off. But some of the assignment instructions were confusing, which made me avoid starting them. Clearer guidelines would have helped me engage more consistently instead of procrastinating out of confusion.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. You can be clicking around, watching videos, exploring the platform, but if you're not in the right headspace to actually do the work, nothing gets submitted. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just avoiding the hard stuff by doing the easy stuff.
Q21: That also kind of fits me. I did a lot of studying offline that didn't show up in the system, and I managed to pass even with low assignment completion. If you understand the material through other means, you can do okay on exams without the LMS showing much activity. The log doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you're clicking on, but they miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just scrolling. They also miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when the material interests you and you want to dig deeper. For me, I was engaged in bursts but not consistently throughout the course.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually thought about the course material outside of the platform. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises I did to manage my mood, or the times I explained concepts to friends. It also wouldn't show that despite my spotty attendance and completion rates, I was genuinely happy and interested in parts of the course.
